medications.indication_rationale: The source clinician prescribed oral amoxicillin as cover against secondary bacterial pneumonia given the retained secretions and high aspiration/infection risk in a decompensating neuromuscular patient. It treats the infection risk, NOT the ventilatory failure, and must never substitute for restoring ventilation and clearing secretions. Dose is NOT specified in the source and is deliberately not synthesised (paediatric patient) — the prescribing dose is flagged for in-person/specialist review.

Applied before the BiPAP mask, saline nasal spray relieves the nasal congestion that caused the patient to remove the mask overnight, improving mask tolerance so nocturnal ventilation is maintained — directly addressing the reversible precipitant.

Giving oxygen alone to correct the SpO2 in neuromuscular hypercapnic failure is dangerous: it can mask ongoing hypoventilation and blunt respiratory drive, worsening CO2 retention and precipitating narcosis. Oxygen, if used, must be titrated alongside ventilation (BiPAP) in a monitored/emergency setting — not as a home fix.

medications.drug_name: sedative or opioid analgesia for the headache
medications.indication_rationale: Sedatives or opioids for the headache or distress cause respiratory depression, which in an already-hypoventilating neuromuscular patient can precipitate respiratory arrest. The headache is treated by reversing the hypercapnia (ventilation), not by sedating analgesia.

The problem is a mechanical pump failure with secretion retention, not bronchospasm; a bronchodilator does not clear secretions or ventilate and must not be mistaken for the treatment. At most a minor adjunct if genuine wheeze coexists.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear the BiPAP mask for ALL sleep including daytime naps, every night, without removing it — especially during any cold.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Nocturnal non-invasive ventilation is what breathes for the fatigued respiratory muscles and prevents CO2 build-up; removing the mask overnight was the direct precipitant of this episode.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Perform three full cycles on the cough-assist (mechanical insufflation-exsufflation) device every 4 hours during any respiratory illness to strip secretions before they consolidate.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Weak cough cannot clear secretions unaided; scheduled mechanical clearance prevents mucus plugging and lobar collapse.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use saline nasal spray before applying the mask each night to keep the nose clear and the mask tolerable.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Nasal congestion drove mask removal; keeping the airway clear protects mask adherence and therefore ventilation.

patient_education_points.point: The severe morning headache and exhaustion happen because weak breathing muscles let carbon dioxide build up overnight — the BiPAP machine breathes for those tired muscles and blows the CO2 back off.
The cough-assist machine acts like artificial chest muscles to blast trapped phlegm out; during a cold it must be used strictly, because a weak cough cannot shift mucus on its own.
This is an emergency situation for someone with this condition — because we cannot measure the CO2 or clear the chest remotely, he needs urgent emergency assessment now, not home-only management.
Do not give oxygen on its own, and do not give sedatives or strong painkillers for the headache — both can make the breathing worse.
Start the prescribed antibiotics as directed once dosing is confirmed, and keep using the breathing mask and cough machine as above.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Urgent community respiratory physiotherapy for daily review of airway clearance technique and cough-assist optimisation during the illness.

follow_up_plan.interval: immediate emergency assessment now; if stabilised, respiratory/neuromuscular team review and daily community respiratory physiotherapy; a GP telehealth review within ~12 hours only as an adjunct to (not instead of) emergency assessment
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: oxygen saturations, work of breathing, CO2 (blood gas), secretion clearance, resolution of the morning headache, and response to BiPAP
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: SpO2 falling below 90% despite BiPAP, returning or worsening headache, increasing drowsiness or difficulty waking, or inability to clear secretions

must_include_items: Recognise acute decompensated (type 2 / hypercapnic) respiratory failure rather than dismissing symptoms as 'part of the muscular dystrophy' or 'just a cold' (diagnostic overshadowing)
Instruct the family to immediately resume BiPAP and use their own cough-assist device as a bridge
Escalate for urgent emergency assessment (call 000 / ambulance) given SpO2 90%, impending hypercapnic coma and inability to clear secretions remotely
Explicitly avoid giving supplemental oxygen alone and avoid sedatives/opioids for the headache
Address nocturnal BiPAP adherence and the nasal-congestion precipitant
acceptable_alternatives: If the neuromuscular/respiratory specialist team is immediately contactable and directs a specific home-intensification protocol with close monitoring, that pathway is acceptable provided emergency escalation remains on the table and SpO2/CO2 concerns are covered.
errors_of_omission: Attributing the presentation to the underlying disability and not escalating (diagnostic overshadowing)
Failing to recognise the morning headache as a hypercapnia marker
Failing to advise mechanical airway clearance / BiPAP resumption
errors_of_commission: Recommending supplemental oxygen alone to correct the SpO2 (can worsen hypercapnia)
Giving a sedative or opioid for the headache (respiratory depression)
Treating an isolated headache with analgesia and closing the encounter
Substituting a salbutamol inhaler or antibiotic for ventilation and secretion clearance
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognition of respiratory decompensation AND emergency escalation AND bridging airway-clearance/BiPAP advice. Missing the decompensation (overshadowing) or failing to escalate is a critical failure regardless of other content. Recommending oxygen alone or sedation is an automatic-fail commission error.
